title: Predicting protein-protein interactions from primary structure
summary: Seminal early paper addressing prediction of protein-protein interactions directly from primary structure and associated data. Used SVM with physicochemical features extracted from protein sequences to classify interacting vs. non-interacting pairs. One of the first systematic ML approaches to PPI prediction from sequences.
datasets: Yeast two-hybrid data, DIP

title: STRING: a database of predicted functional associations between proteins
summary: Original STRING database publication. Provides integrated protein-protein interaction predictions with confidence scores from multiple genomic context methods (coexpression, gene fusion, genome context). Covers 261,033 orthologs from 89 genomes with expected accuracy of at least 80% at confidence threshold. Foundational resource for functional PPI analysis.
datasets: STRING v1 (261,033 proteins, 89 genomes)

title: Kernel methods for predicting protein-protein interactions
summary: Proposed pairwise kernel methods for predicting protein-protein interactions using multiple data sources including protein sequences, Gene Ontology annotations, local network properties, and homologous interactions. Introduced the pairwise kernel that converts a single-protein kernel into a protein-pair kernel for SVM classification. Applied to yeast using BIND database.
datasets: BIND yeast PPI database, DIP, MIPS

title: Predicting protein-protein interactions based only on sequences information
venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
summary: Developed conjoint triad method for PPI prediction using only sequence information. Groups 20 amino acids into 7 classes based on dipoles and side chain volumes, then represents protein pairs using a 343-dimensional conjoint triad descriptor. Combined with SVM kernel function to achieve prediction accuracy of ~83.9% on human PPIs. Widely cited as a landmark sequence-only approach.
datasets: Human PPI dataset (~16,000 pairs), H. pylori dataset

title: Using support vector machine combined with auto covariance to predict protein-protein interactions from protein sequences
summary: Introduced auto covariance (AC) descriptor combined with SVM for sequence-based PPI prediction. AC captures interactions between residues a certain distance apart in the sequence (up to 30 adjacent amino acids), overcoming the limitation of the conjoint triad method which only considers nearest neighbors. Achieved 88.09% prediction accuracy on independent yeast PPI dataset.
datasets: S. cerevisiae DIP data (5943 pairs training, 11474 test)

title: Accurate prediction of protein-protein interactions from sequence alignments using a Bayesian method
summary: Bayesian network method that predicts interaction partners directly from multiple alignments of amino acid sequences without tunable parameters or training examples. Applied to bacterial two-component signaling systems, achieving high accuracy in reconstructing interaction networks across sequenced bacteria. Also demonstrated on polyketide synthases.
datasets: Bacterial two-component signaling systems across sequenced genomes

title: The Negatome database: a reference set of non-interacting protein pairs
summary: Negatome: collection of protein and domain pairs unlikely to engage in direct physical interaction. Derived from manual literature curation and analysis of protein complexes with known 3D structure. Less biased toward functionally dissimilar proteins than random negative sets based on cellular localization. Essential resource for training and evaluating PPI prediction algorithms.
datasets: Negatome v1 (manually curated and structure-derived non-interacting pairs)

title: SKEMPI: a Structural Kinetic and Energetic database of Mutant Protein Interactions
summary: Original SKEMPI database: 3047 binding free energy changes upon mutation for 158 protein-protein complex structures from PDB. More than four times more data than previously collected. Used to investigate structural principles governing how mutations influence PPI binding and for training empirical binding affinity scoring functions.
datasets: SKEMPI v1 (3047 ΔΔG measurements, 85 protein-protein complexes)

title: Negatome 2.0: a database of non-interacting proteins derived by literature mining, manual annotation and protein structure analysis
summary: Major update of Negatome with >300% growth in content. Introduced advanced text mining (modified Excerbt tool with semantic sentence analysis) to guide manual annotation of non-interacting protein pairs. Negatome 2.0 provides a critical resource for negative training data in PPI prediction methods.
datasets: Negatome 2.0 (literature + structure-derived, grown 300%+ from v1)

title: Predicting protein–protein interactions from sequences using evolutionary profiles and profile-kernel SVMs
summary: This paper introduces a systematic difficulty classification framework for PPI prediction (C1/C2/C3 classes) and proposes a new method using evolutionary profiles with profile-kernel SVMs. It significantly improves over the state-of-the-art especially for C3 class proteins (sequence-dissimilar to training data), pushing AUC from 59% to 67% for human C3 interactions. The study definitively showed that most computational PPI methods fail on out-of-distribution proteins and established benchmark standards for cross-validation that account for sequence similarity bias. Gene expression filtering further increases accuracy for the most reliable interactions. The C1/C2/C3 difficulty classification has become a standard benchmark protocol adopted by subsequent PPI prediction papers to ensure fair evaluation and prevent overoptimistic reporting from sequence-similar train-test splits.
datasets: PINA human PPI (v3/2010), yeast PPI, UniProt sequences

title: STRING v10: protein–protein interaction networks, integrated over the tree of life
summary: STRING v10 covers more than 2000 organisms with protein-protein interaction data. Introduced novel algorithms for scaling to large numbers of organisms. Provides comprehensive scoring and integration of both direct physical and indirect functional interactions.
datasets: STRING v10 (>2000 organisms)

title: Boosting Prediction of Protein–Protein Interactions with Deep Neural Networks
venue: Journal of Chemical Information and Modeling
summary: DeepPPI is a deep neural network method for protein-protein interaction prediction that learns discriminative features from common protein sequence descriptors using two architectures: DeepPPI-Sep (two separate networks per protein) and DeepPPI-Con (single concatenated network). The model automatically extracts high-level features through layer-wise abstraction without manual feature engineering. Tested on human and yeast PPI datasets, DeepPPI achieves accuracy of 92.50%, precision 94.38%, recall 90.56%, MCC 85.08%, and AUC 97.43%, outperforming existing methods based on SVM and other classifiers at the time of publication. The two-step architecture (DeepPPI-Sep and DeepPPI-Con) highlights that training two separate networks per protein before combining enables better feature disentanglement compared to a single concatenated network trained end-to-end.
datasets: Pan's human PPI dataset, S. cerevisiae dataset from DIP

title: Accurate prediction of protein–protein interactions by integrating potential evolutionary information embedded in PSSM profile and discriminative vector machine classifier
summary: PSSM-based method for PPI prediction using an Information-theoretic Wavelet Lifting Descriptor (IWLD) to capture evolutionary information from position-specific scoring matrices combined with a Discriminative Vector Machine (DVM) classifier. Applied to Yeast and H. pylori datasets. One of several influential papers in the PSSM+SVM tradition for sequence-based PPI prediction.
datasets: S. cerevisiae yeast PPI dataset, H. pylori dataset

title: Predicting protein–protein interactions through sequence-based deep learning
summary: This paper presents DPPI, a deep learning framework for protein-protein interaction prediction using sequence information alone. The method employs a deep Siamese-like convolutional neural network combined with random projection and data augmentation, leveraging PSSM evolutionary profiles. DPPI outperforms state-of-the-art methods by 15-20% in terms of auPR and is 15 times computationally faster. The model predicts both heterodimeric and homodimeric interactions and was applied to cytokine-receptor binding affinity prediction. A key innovation is the random projection module that explores combinations of protein motifs from two sequence profiles. The model uses random projection to map learned protein motif representations to independent spaces, enabling efficient handling of 100,000+ training PPIs while remaining competitive for cytokine-receptor binding affinity estimation.
datasets: Human PPI dataset, S. cerevisiae dataset, cytokine-receptor binding dataset

title: Multifaceted protein–protein interaction prediction based on Siamese residual RCNN
summary: PIPR (Protein-Protein Interaction Prediction based on Siamese Residual RCNN) is an end-to-end deep learning framework for PPI prediction from protein sequences alone. The model uses a residual recurrent convolutional neural network in a Siamese architecture, pre-trains amino acid embeddings to capture physicochemical properties, and leverages both local features and contextualized sequential information. Evaluated on binary PPI prediction, interaction type classification, and binding affinity estimation, PIPR outperforms state-of-the-art systems especially on multi-species datasets. A key strength is its ability to generalize to different PPI task types without significant reengineering. The Siamese architecture captures mutual influence between protein pairs without requiring predefined interaction features, and PIPR is the first method to unify binary classification, interaction type prediction, and binding affinity estimation in one framework.
datasets: Guo yeast dataset, multi-species dataset (H. pylori, C. elegans, H. sapiens), SHS27k, SHS148k

title: STRING v11: protein–protein association networks with increased coverage, supporting functional discovery in genome-wide experimental datasets
summary: STRING version 11 update more than doubling the number of organisms covered. Covers >1100 organisms with comprehensive protein-protein association data combining physical and functional interactions from multiple sources. Added novel scalable algorithms for transferring interaction information between organisms.
datasets: STRING v11 (>1100 organisms)

title: SE(3)-Transformers: 3D Roto-Translation Equivariant Attention Networks
summary: SE(3)-Transformers combine the expressiveness of attention mechanisms with SE(3)-equivariance for 3D point cloud and molecular structure processing. Applied to protein structure tasks including interaction prediction. Key building block for subsequent equivariant architecture advances including Invariant Point Attention in AlphaFold2.
datasets: QM9, N-body particle simulations, protein structure benchmarks

title: D-SCRIPT translates genome to phenome with sequence-based, structure-aware, genome-scale predictions of protein–protein interactions
summary: D-SCRIPT is a deep learning model for predicting physical protein-protein interactions from amino acid sequences using a structurally motivated design. The model uses a pre-trained protein language model to generate rich representations, then infers inter-protein contact maps as an intermediate structural output. This approach improves cross-species generalizability since protein structure is more evolutionarily conserved than sequence. A D-SCRIPT model trained on 38,345 human PPIs achieves improved functional characterization of fly proteins, predicts genome-wide PPIs in cow, and its contact map output correlates with known 3D complex structures. The structurally-motivated contact map intermediate output provides interpretability absent in most black-box deep learning models, allowing biologists to inspect predicted contacting residue pairs and assess biological plausibility of predicted interactions.
datasets: Human PPI (BioGRID), D. melanogaster interactome, B. taurus proteome, STRING

title: Fast end-to-end learning on protein surfaces
summary: dMaSIF (differentiable MaSIF) introduces point-cloud-based operators for protein surface learning that replace the expensive mesh generation of MaSIF. Achieves 10-100x speedups while maintaining competitive accuracy for PPI site prediction, protein-protein interaction matching, and protein-small molecule binding. Uses implicit surface representations and orientation-aware point convolutions.
datasets: MaSIF-site, MaSIF-search, PDB benchmarks

title: Invariant Point Attention and SE(3)-equivariant structure modules in AlphaFold2
summary: AlphaFold2's structure module introduces Invariant Point Attention (IPA), a geometry-aware SE(3)-invariant attention mechanism that processes 3D protein backbone frames. IPA enables the model to reason about spatial relationships between residues while maintaining rotational and translational invariance, a crucial architectural contribution applied broadly in protein interaction modeling.
datasets: PDB, CASP14 benchmark

title: Topsy-Turvy: integrating a global view into sequence-based PPI prediction
summary: Topsy-Turvy synthesizes sequence-based bottom-up and network topology top-down approaches in a multi-scale deep learning model for PPI prediction. Using a transfer-learning framework, it incorporates global network patterns from GLIDE (a spectral link predictor) during training while making predictions from sequence data only. This multi-objective training balances structural and topological considerations. In cross-species settings, Topsy-Turvy achieves state-of-the-art performance on five model organisms for genome-scale PPI prediction. The TT-Hybrid variant, combining Topsy-Turvy with GLIDE link predictions, further outperforms constituent components for species with existing PPI data, addressing both well-characterized and poorly-characterized proteins. Topsy-Turvy is feasible for whole-genome screens where structure-based methods like AlphaFold-Multimer would be computationally infeasible.
datasets: Human, E. coli, D. melanogaster, C. elegans, S. cerevisiae STRING PPI networks

title: Deep learning frameworks for protein–protein interaction prediction
summary: This comprehensive review paper covers deep learning architectures applied to PPI prediction including CNNs, RNNs, graph neural networks, transformers, and autoencoders. The paper benchmarks major methods including DPPI, PIPR, D-SCRIPT, and graph-based approaches on standardized datasets. It discusses feature embedding strategies from amino acid composition to protein language models, benchmark datasets from DIP/BioGRID, and extended applications in drug discovery and functional annotation. A key contribution is the comparative analysis showing that sequence-based methods still lag structure-aware and network-aware approaches. The review provides guidance on appropriate benchmarking protocols to avoid data leakage through sequence similarity, and identifies the transition from hand-crafted features to learned representations as the defining advance in the field.
datasets: Survey of multiple PPI datasets from DIP, BioGRID, IntAct, STRING

title: State-of-the-art computational methods to predict protein–protein interactions with high accuracy and coverage
summary: This comprehensive review covers sequence-based, structure-based, and co-abundance-based computational approaches for PPI prediction, with particular focus on how deep learning advances each data type. The paper organizes methods taxonomically by primary data source and evaluates their strengths and weaknesses. Key analyses include the impact of AlphaFold structures on structure-based prediction, limitations of co-expression features, and the challenge of integrating multi-modal data. The review identifies that network-aware methods combining sequence and interaction topology consistently achieve the highest accuracy and coverage. Special attention is given to the shift from PSSM features to protein language model embeddings and how this transition has reshaped performance benchmarks across all prediction paradigms.
datasets: Review of multiple PPI databases and benchmarks

title: PeSTo: parameter-free geometric deep learning for accurate prediction of protein binding interfaces
summary: PeSTo is a parameter-free geometric transformer that accurately identifies protein binding interfaces from experimentally solved and predicted (AlphaFold) structures. Processes protein atomic coordinates and element types without requiring alignment or sequence profiles, enabling efficient interface prediction across the growing foldome.
datasets: PDB protein complexes, AlphaFold predicted structures

title: ProteinMAE: masked autoencoder for protein surface self-supervised learning
summary: ProteinMAE introduces a masked autoencoder pre-training strategy for protein molecular surfaces, addressing label scarcity in surface-based protein learning. The model learns informative surface representations capturing chemical and geometric properties, then fine-tunes for PPI prediction and protein design tasks with limited labels.
datasets: PDB molecular surfaces, MaSIF benchmarks

title: G-RANK: an equivariant graph neural network for the scoring of protein-protein docking models
summary: G-RANK applies GVP-GNN (Geometric Vector Perceptron Graph Neural Network) to score protein-protein docking models. Uses equivariant message passing to evaluate candidate docked structures and rank them by nativeness. Achieves strong performance identifying near-native protein-protein complex structures from docking decoy sets.
datasets: Docking Benchmark 5.5, ZDOCK decoy sets

title: De novo design of protein structure and function with RFdiffusion
summary: RFdiffusion fine-tunes RoseTTAFold on protein structure denoising tasks to obtain a generative model for protein backbones. Achieves outstanding performance on de novo binder design, symmetric oligomer design, enzyme active site scaffolding, and motif scaffolding. Experimentally validates hundreds of designed symmetric assemblies, metal-binding proteins, and binders with cryo-EM structure confirmation.
datasets: PDB training structures, experimental validation datasets

title: The STRING database in 2023: protein–protein association networks and functional enrichment analyses for any sequenced genome of interest
summary: Major update of the STRING database, the premier resource for protein-protein association networks. Covers 67 million proteins from 14,094 organisms. Integrates physical and functional interactions from text mining, computational predictions, co-expression, and experimental data. Adds functional enrichment analysis capabilities for any sequenced genome.
datasets: STRING v12 (67M proteins, 14094 organisms)

title: UniProt: the Universal Protein Knowledgebase in 2023
summary: Latest major update of UniProt Knowledgebase, the definitive resource for protein sequences and functional annotation. Contains >227 million sequences and >85% now linked to AlphaFold structure predictions. Used as the primary sequence source for virtually all computational PPI prediction methods. Reviewed (Swiss-Prot) entries provide gold-standard sequence and functional annotation.
datasets: UniProtKB (>227M sequences, >85% with AlphaFold structures)

title: Machine learning on protein–protein interaction prediction: models, challenges and trends
summary: Comprehensive survey of machine learning-based PPI prediction methods from 2020-2023. Reviews data sources, protein feature representations, ML model types (sequence-based, structure-based, hybrid), current challenges (data quality, negative sampling, generalizability), and future directions including using AlphaFold-predicted structures. Essential reference for state of the field.
datasets: N/A (review paper)

title: Structural biology: The transformational era
summary: Editorial review by Shoshana Wodak on the transformational impact of AI (AlphaFold, RoseTTAFold) on structural biology and protein-protein interaction prediction. Reviews implications for improved PPI prediction benchmarking and the new era of structure-based interactome analysis. From a leading authority on protein docking and interaction prediction.
datasets: N/A (review/editorial)

title: Cracking the black box of deep sequence-based protein–protein interaction prediction
summary: This critical benchmark paper systematically investigates how much deep learning PPI prediction models depend on data leakage and sequence homology artifacts. The authors find that many high-accuracy estimates are inflated due to train-test sequence similarity, and demonstrate that models primarily learn to recognize sequence similarity to known interactors rather than genuine interaction features. The paper introduces rigorous cross-validation protocols that prevent sequence-based leakage and shows that truly sequence-independent PPI prediction remains an unsolved challenge. Reproducible models are released to enable fair benchmarking. The authors release reproducible implementations with standardized benchmarking code, establishing a rigorous framework that other researchers can use for fair method comparison and setting a new standard for the field.
datasets: Human, yeast, and multiple organism PPI networks from BioGRID, IntAct, STRING

title: Exploiting Hierarchical Interactions for Protein Surface Learning
summary: HCGNet (Hierarchical Chemical and Geometric Feature Interaction Network) bridges chemical and geometric features with hierarchical interactions for protein surface analysis. Captures atom-level chemical features and hierarchical surface-atom residue interactions. Outperforms MaSIF by 2.3% on site prediction and 3.2% on interaction matching tasks.
datasets: MaSIF site and search benchmarks, PDB protein surfaces

title: Generalized Biomolecular Modeling and Design with RoseTTAFold All-Atom
summary: RFAA extends RoseTTAFold to model full biological assemblies containing proteins, nucleic acids, small molecules, metals, and covalent modifications. Fine-tuned to develop RFdiffusionAA which generates protein binding pockets directly around small molecules. Designs and validates proteins binding digoxigenin, heme, and bilin molecules with accurate cryo-EM structures.
datasets: PDB full biological assemblies, experimental validation structures

title: Out of Many, One: Designing and Scaffolding Proteins at the Scale of the Structural Universe with Genie 2
summary: Genie 2 extends the original Genie diffusion model to design complex proteins engaging multiple interaction partners and performing multiple functions. Trained on the entire AlphaFold structural universe, achieves state-of-the-art performance on unconditional and conditional protein backbone generation including motif scaffolding and protein complex design.
datasets: AlphaFold DB structural universe, PDB

title: Unified Sampling and Ranking for Protein Docking with DFMDock
summary: DFMDock unifies structure generation and decoy ranking in protein-protein docking via denoising force matching. Predicts both denoising forces and scalar energy trained via force matching and energy contrastive objectives. Outperforms DiffDock-PP with 44% sampling success vs 8% on Docking Benchmark 5.5 and produces funnel-shaped energy landscapes.
datasets: Docking Benchmark 5.5, DIPS pre-training dataset

title: AI-guided pipeline for protein–protein interaction drug discovery identifies a SARS-CoV-2 inhibitor
summary: Comprehensive pipeline combining experimental and computational tools to identify and validate PPI targets for early-stage drug discovery. Developed a multi-adaptive SVM (maSVM) classifier to prioritize interactions using quantitative PPI assay data and AlphaFold-Multimer predictions. Applied to SARS-CoV-2 proteins to identify high-confidence interactions, then used VirtualFlow for ultra-large virtual drug screening of the NSP10-NSP16 methyltransferase complex, identifying a compound that disrupts the complex and SARS-CoV-2 replication.
datasets: LuTHy binary PPI assay data, AlphaFold-Multimer predictions, VirtualFlow compound library

title: Accurate structure prediction of biomolecular interactions with AlphaFold 3
summary: AlphaFold 3 predicts the joint structure of complexes including proteins, nucleic acids, small molecules, ions, and modified residues using a diffusion-based architecture. Significantly advances protein-protein and protein-ligand interaction prediction accuracy. Released as a web server and later partially open-sourced under restricted license (AlphaFold Server).
datasets: PDB structures, CCD ligand library, nucleic acids data

title: Boltz-1: Democratizing Biomolecular Interaction Modeling
summary: Boltz-1 is an open-source deep learning model achieving AlphaFold3-level accuracy in predicting 3D structures of biomolecular complexes. Incorporates innovations in model architecture, speed optimization, and data processing. Introduces Boltz-steering, an inference-time steering technique that fixes hallucinations and non-physical predictions. Released training/inference code, model weights, datasets, and benchmarks under MIT license.
datasets: PDB (training), custom biomolecular interaction benchmarks

title: Technical Report of HelixFold3 for Biomolecular Structure Prediction
summary: Replicates AlphaFold3 capabilities via the PaddleHelix team. HelixFold3 provides precise predictions for diverse protein complex structures, especially therapeutic protein interactions. Achieves remarkable success in antigen-antibody and peptide-protein structure prediction, greatly surpassing AlphaFold 3 on antigen-antibody interactions. Available publicly on the PaddleHelix platform with both general and antigen-antibody versions.
datasets: PDB structures, antibody-antigen structures

title: Foundation models in molecular biology
summary: Comprehensive review of foundation models in molecular biology, covering RNA sequence, DNA sequence, protein sequence, single-cell transcriptome, and spatial transcriptome models. Discusses research directions for development of foundation models in molecular biology. Contextualizes foundation models in PPI prediction, protein structure prediction, and functional genomics.
datasets: Review (no datasets)

title: Benchmarking AlphaFold3's protein-protein complex accuracy and machine learning prediction reliability for binding free energy changes upon mutation
summary: Independent validation of AlphaFold 3 predictions using SKEMPI 2.0 database (317 protein-protein complexes, 8338 mutations). AF3 complex structures give Pearson correlation of 0.86 for binding free energy changes upon mutation, slightly less than 0.88 from PDB structures. AF3 structures led to 8.6% increase in prediction RMSE compared to original PDB structures, and are not reliable for intrinsically flexible regions.
datasets: SKEMPI 2.0 database (317 complexes, 8338 mutations)

title: SpatialPPI: Three-dimensional space protein-protein interaction prediction with AlphaFold Multimer
summary: Proposes SpatialPPI, a highly accurate method for predicting protein-protein interactions using 3D structural information from AlphaFold Multimer. Uses the AlphaFold Multimer confidence metrics and 3D structural features as inputs to a deep neural network classifier to determine whether two proteins actually interact (binary PPI prediction). Addresses the limitation that AlphaFold Multimer cannot distinguish true interactors from non-interactors.
datasets: Human PPI benchmark datasets, AlphaFold Multimer predictions

title: Revolutionizing protein-protein interaction prediction with deep learning
summary: Reviews latest advances in computational methods for 3D protein complex modeling and prediction of protein interaction partners, emphasizing application of deep learning methods from coevolution analysis. Covers recent developments in AlphaFold-based methods, co-evolutionary approaches, and end-to-end deep learning for PPI prediction. Highlights biomedical applications of PPI prediction and outlines challenges in the field.
datasets: Review (no datasets)

title: Deep learning in modeling protein complex structures: From contact prediction to end-to-end approaches
summary: Reviews recent advances in deep learning-based approaches for modeling protein-protein complex structures including inter-protein contact prediction and end-to-end approaches. Covers development from coevolutionary contact methods to AlphaFold-based modeling and other end-to-end deep learning frameworks. Discusses advantages, limitations, challenges, and future directions for protein complex structure prediction.
datasets: Review (no datasets)

title: Chai-1: Decoding the molecular interactions of life
summary: Chai-1 is a multi-modal foundation model for molecular structure prediction achieving state-of-the-art accuracy across tasks relevant to drug discovery. It can be prompted with experimental restraints (e.g., from wet-lab data) for double-digit percentage point accuracy boosts, and operates in single-sequence mode (without MSAs) while preserving most performance. Model weights and inference code are released as a Python package for non-commercial use and via a free commercial web interface. Represents a key open-source alternative to AlphaFold3 for biomolecular complex structure prediction including PPI.
datasets: PDB, CASP evaluation sets, CAMEO, custom drug discovery benchmarks

title: OpenFold: retraining AlphaFold2 yields new insights into its learning mechanisms and capacity for generalization
summary: Reports OpenFold, a fast, memory-efficient, and fully trainable open-source reimplementation of AlphaFold2. Training from scratch on OpenProteinSet, OpenFold matches AlphaFold2 accuracy. By systematically reducing training set size and diversity, the authors reveal that OpenFold generalizes robustly even with highly limited data. Analysis of intermediate training structures uncovers a hierarchical learning mechanism: spatial dimensions (1D → 2D → 3D structure) are learned sequentially. OpenFold is a critical resource enabling new tasks such as protein-ligand complex structure prediction and studying PPI interfaces, with direct relevance to the broader PPI modeling community.
datasets: PDB, OpenProteinSet (largest public MSA database), CAMEO, CASP15 evaluation sets

title: Multi-Modal Topology-Aware Graph Neural Network for Robust Chemical-Protein Interaction Prediction
summary: MM-TCoCPIn integrates three causally grounded modalities for chemical-protein interaction prediction: network topology (CTC encoder), biomedical semantics (SciBERT), and 3D protein structure (GVP-GNN on AlphaFold2 contact graphs). Achieves AUC=0.93 with each modality contributing distinct biological insight via counterfactual and ablation analyses.
datasets: STITCH, STRING, PubMed protein-chemical interaction datasets

title: A Hierarchical Adaptive Diffusion Model for Flexible Protein-Protein Docking
summary: Proposes hierarchical adaptive diffusion separating global rigid-body motions and local intra-protein flexibility for flexible protein-protein docking. Adapts local flexibility schedule based on predicted conformational change level (induced-fit effect). Couples local and global diffusion processes through sequence, evolution, structure, and dynamics features. Outperforms DiffDock-PP and GeoDock on DB5.5.
datasets: DIPS-AF pre-training dataset, Docking Benchmark 5.5

title: The STRING database in 2025: protein networks with directionality of regulation
summary: Latest STRING update (2025) adding directionality of regulatory interactions. Compiles, scores and integrates protein-protein association information from experimental assays, computational predictions and prior knowledge. Includes both physical and functional interactions for comprehensive networks.
datasets: STRING v12.0 with regulatory directionality data

title: Structure-Based Approaches for Protein–Protein Interaction Prediction Using Machine Learning and Deep Learning
summary: Recent review (2025) of structure-based computational approaches for PPI prediction using ML and deep learning. Covers how 3D structural information improves predictive accuracy for functional PPI sites. Discusses methods leveraging docking, interface feature extraction, and structure-augmented deep learning models. Summary of advances in the AlphaFold era.
datasets: N/A (review paper)

title: Recent progress and future challenges in structure-based protein-protein interaction prediction
summary: 2025 review of recent progress in computational methods for modeling protein complexes and predicting PPIs based on 3D structures. Covers template-based docking, AI-based methods (AlphaFold-Multimer, RoseTTAFold2), and de novo structure prediction for PPI. Discusses remaining challenges for unresolved and disordered interactions.
datasets: N/A (review paper)

title: AlphaFold3: An Overview of Applications and Performance Insights
summary: Comprehensive review of AlphaFold3's capabilities for modeling single protein structures, protein complexes, protein-ligand docking, and protein-nucleic acid complexes. Explores AF3's role in drug discovery, epitope prediction, and disease-related mutations. Critically evaluates AF3's performance improvements and limitations including challenges in predicting flexible regions and novel complexes.
datasets: Review (no datasets)

title: Multimodal graph, surface, and language-based model for protein-protein interaction prediction
summary: GSMFormer-PPI integrates protein molecular surface features (via MaSIF), 3D structural graphs, and residue-level sequence embeddings in a multimodal transformer framework for PPI prediction. Employs geometric deep learning for surface descriptors, GCN for structural context, and transformer for cross-modal interactions. Outperforms traditional graph-based models on the PINDER dataset.
datasets: PINDER PPI benchmark dataset

title: Toward the dynamic interactome: it's about time
summary: This seminal review introduces the concept of the dynamic interactome—time-varying protein interaction networks that reflect active signaling and regulatory states. The paper reviews methods for inferring context-specific active subnetworks from expression data overlaid on physical interaction networks. It covers biclustering approaches for identifying co-regulated modules, integration of interaction data with transcriptomics to identify pathway activations, conditional regulatory interaction inference, and multi-time-point analysis methods. The review articulates the distinction between the structural interactome (all possible interactions) and the functional interactome (condition-specific active interactions) and establishes the conceptual foundation for subsequent tissue-specific and single-cell PPI network inference methods. Key challenges including noise in both PPI and expression data are discussed along with proposed solutions.
datasets: Human PPI from various databases, gene expression datasets

title: The STRING database in 2021: customizable protein–protein networks, and functional characterization of user-uploaded gene/measurement sets
summary: STRING v11.5 integrates known and predicted protein-protein associations from text mining, co-expression, experimental data, computational predictions, and interolog transfers across 14,000+ organisms. This update introduces a new scoring mode for physical interactions distinguished from functional associations, and improved text-mining with NLP models trained to recognize protein-complex co-occurrences. Coverage exceeds 67 million proteins. The database enables customizable network construction, functional enrichment analysis on user-uploaded gene sets, and network visualization. A new physical interaction score based on a gold standard of yeast complexes enables distinction of direct binding interactions. Physical interaction scoring distinguishes direct binding from indirect functional associations, enabling more specific analysis of protein complexes. STRING 11.5 covers 67 million proteins across 14,000+ organisms, making it the widest-coverage PPI resource available.
datasets: All public PPI databases (BIND, DIP, GRID, HPRD, IntAct, MINT), text-mined from >3M publications

title: BioPlex 3.0: Networks of interactions among proteins and complexes in cells and proteomes
summary: BioPlex 3.0 is the most comprehensive mass spectrometry-based human PPI map, containing nearly 120,000 interactions among ~15,000 proteins from HEK293T cells, plus ~70,000 interactions from HCT116 cells. Generated by AP-MS of over 10,000 human bait proteins from the ORFeome collection, BioPlex 3.0 identifies thousands of previously unknown interactions and maps entire protein complexes. The network reveals principles of protein complex organization, enables multi-scale cell structure mapping when combined with protein imaging data, and serves as an essential reference interactome for systems biology and drug target identification. BioPlex uses AP-MS with quantitative protein identification enabling detection of sub-stoichiometric interactions, transient associations, and co-complex membership patterns that traditional binary interaction screens miss, creating a richer network topology.
datasets: Human ORFeome collection (~10,128 bait proteins in HEK293T, ~5,522 in HCT116)

title: Efficient link prediction in the protein–protein interaction network using topological information in a generative adversarial network machine learning model
summary: This paper develops a cGAN-based link prediction tool for PPI networks using only raw network topology without requiring molecular node attributes. The data processing framework uses a modified breadth-first search to extract induced subgraphs as image-like representations, which are then fed into a conditional GAN with Wasserstein distance loss and gradient penalty. Evaluated on five species from STRING, the model achieves AUROC=0.915 and NDCG=0.763 on average. This is the first demonstration that a cGAN conditioned on topological features can effectively predict protein interactions without any protein-level information. The image-to-image translation cGAN represents a novel framing of PPI link prediction, treating network topology as pixel-space patterns and leveraging the generator-discriminator framework to capture non-obvious topological regularities.
datasets: STRING PPI networks for 5 species (human, mouse, yeast, E. coli, fly)

title: HumanNet v3: an improved database of human gene networks for disease research
summary: HumanNet v3 is a three-tier human gene/protein network integrating physical PPI (HumanNet-PI), functional gene associations (HumanNet-FN), and literature co-citation-extended network (HumanNet-XC). Built by integrating protein interactions from iRefIndex, BioPlex, BioGRID, and IntAct with inferred co-functional links from co-expression, co-citation, genetic interactions, phylogenetic profiling, and pathway databases. HumanNet v3 outperforms previous versions and other integrated networks for disease gene prediction due to substantially expanded gold standards (from 124,950 to 260,962 gene pairs). The database demonstrates utility for COVID-19 host gene identification, predicting both known and novel disease-associated proteins. Three distinct network tiers allow users to choose appropriate evidence stringency depending on whether they need conserved physical interactions, broad functional associations, or maximum coverage.
datasets: BioPlex 1-3, BioGRID, iRefIndex, IntAct, KEGG, Reactome, PubMed

title: PrePPI: A Structure Informed Proteome-wide Database of Protein–Protein Interactions
summary: PrePPI is an updated proteome-wide PPI prediction database that combines structural and non-structural evidence in a Bayesian framework. The structural modeling (SM) component uses template-based modeling, now leveraging AlphaFold structures parsed into domains, with a unique scoring function for evaluating putative complexes. Non-structural features include co-expression, phylogenetic profiles, and functional annotations. For the human proteome, PrePPI contains ~1.3 million predicted interactions with likelihood ratios. The database achieves excellent ROC performance versus E. coli and human experimental PPI databases, and is accessible as a searchable web server. The integration of AlphaFold2 domain structures replaces in-house homology models, substantially expanding structural coverage and enabling more accurate template-based complex modeling for proteins previously lacking experimental structures.
datasets: Human proteome, AlphaFold structure database, multiple PPI databases for validation

title: A tissue-specific atlas of protein–protein associations enables disease gene prioritization
summary: This paper presents a tissue-specific protein association atlas derived from coabundance of proteins in 7,811 human biopsies, scoring likelihood of 116 million protein associations across 11 tissues. The atlas uses proteomics (mass spectrometry) rather than transcriptomics to capture actual protein coabundance patterns. It recovers known protein complexes, maps relationships between disease genes and cellular components, and provides tissue-relevant disease gene associations for genetic variants. Analysis reveals that signaling interactions are less commonly shared across tissues than stable protein complexes, highlighting the importance of tissue-specific context for disease gene interpretation. The tissue-specific atlas reveals that only ~20% of protein pairs known to interact in STRING are confirmed as likely associations in the brain, highlighting the importance of tissue context for interpreting protein interaction databases.
datasets: Human biopsy proteomics (7,811 samples across 11 tissues), STRING, BioPlex for validation

title: Prediction of protein-protein interaction sites using an ensemble method
summary: This paper presents an ensemble learning method for PPI site prediction combining SVM, decision tree, and Naive Bayes classifiers with majority voting. Features include sequence profiles (PSSM), secondary structure, solvent accessibility, and evolutionary conservation. The ensemble outperforms each individual classifier and competes with contemporary methods. An important contribution is the systematic analysis of feature importance showing that PSSM evolutionary information provides the largest discriminative power for interface residue identification. The paper also highlights the challenge of class imbalance (typically only 15-20% of surface residues are interface residues) and proposes resampling strategies. The study finds PSSM evolutionary conservation dominates discriminative power across all classifiers, explaining why later deep learning methods that extract PSSM features with CNN/RNN architectures achieve substantial improvements over these shallow baselines.
datasets: Dset_164 from heterodimeric protein complexes in PDB

title: Deciphering interaction fingerprints from protein molecular surfaces using geometric deep learning
summary: MaSIF (Molecular Surface Interaction Fingerprinting) presents a conceptual framework using geometric deep learning to learn interaction fingerprints from protein molecular surfaces represented as point clouds. The method decomposes protein surfaces into local patches and learns non-Euclidean surface descriptors encoding chemical and geometric properties. MaSIF addresses three challenges: protein pocket-ligand prediction (MaSIF-ligand), protein-protein interaction site prediction (MaSIF-site), and ultrafast scanning of protein surfaces for complex prediction (MaSIF-search). On PPI site prediction, MaSIF-site achieves AUC 0.87, significantly outperforming sequence-based methods and enabling surface-based drug discovery. MaSIF represents a paradigm shift from describing proteins by sequence or atomic structure to describing them by surface geometry and chemistry, enabling faster surface scanning (~0.1 seconds per protein pair) compared to structure-based docking approaches.
datasets: PDB protein structures (~11,000 for search), PPI benchmark datasets

title: Protein-protein interaction site prediction through combining local contextual and global sequence features
summary: DeepPPISP is an end-to-end deep learning framework for PPI site prediction that combines local contextual and global sequence features for the first time. For local features, a sliding window (size 7) captures neighboring amino acid properties; for global features, a text-CNN extracts patterns from the entire protein sequence. The combination of local and global features improves F-measure by ~4% and MCC by ~9% over local-only methods. Benchmarked against PSIVER, SPPIDER, SPRINGS, ISIS, and RF_PPI on standard datasets, DeepPPISP achieves state-of-the-art performance with ablation studies confirming the importance of each feature type. The model's ablation experiments show that removing global features reduces MCC by 9% while removing PSSM local features also substantially degrades performance, confirming the complementary nature of local sequential and global compositional representations.
datasets: Dset_72, Dset_186 benchmark datasets from heterodimeric proteins

title: Structure-aware protein–protein interaction site prediction using deep graph convolutional network
summary: GraphPPIS reframes PPI site prediction as a graph node classification problem using deep graph convolutional networks with initial residual and identity mapping techniques. The protein graph represents amino acids as nodes with edges based on spatial distances (14Å cutoff). An 8-layer architecture captures information from high-order spatial neighbors, showing that deeper architectures allow significant performance improvement. GraphPPIS outperforms sequence-based and structure-based methods by >12.5% AUPRC and >10.5% MCC. Predicted interacting sites are more spatially clustered and closer to native interfaces even when false-positive predictions are made. The depth of 8 graph convolutional layers is shown to be crucial—with each additional layer propagating information further in spatial neighborhoods—and the initial residual connections prevent over-smoothing that degrades performance in deep GCNs.
datasets: Test_60 and Test_315 benchmark datasets, Dset_186 training set

title: PIPENN: Protein Interface Prediction from Sequence with an Ensemble of Neural Nets
summary: PIPENN systematically explores six deep learning architectures (CNN, ResNet, RNN, LSTM, U-Net, and ensemble) for predicting protein interface residues from sequence-derived features. The study introduces the BioDL benchmark covering protein-protein, protein-nucleotide, and protein-small molecule interfaces. The ensemble of all six architectures achieves the best performance across interaction types. Protein language model embeddings (ProtT5-XL) substantially improve predictions beyond traditional PSSM-based features. The paper provides the first comprehensive comparison of DL architectures for multi-partner interface prediction, establishing design principles for sequence-based interface predictors. The BioDL benchmark covering three interaction partner types (protein, nucleotide, small molecule) in a unified evaluation framework enables fair multi-partner comparison and reveals that protein-protein interfaces are harder to predict from sequence alone than nucleotide-binding sites.
datasets: BioDL (PDB protein-protein interactions, BioLip DNA/RNA/small molecule interactions)

title: Deep Learning for Predicting Biomolecular Binding Sites of Proteins
summary: This comprehensive review covers the rapidly evolving field of deep learning methods for predicting protein-biomolecule binding sites including protein-protein, protein-DNA/RNA, protein-peptide, and protein-small molecule interfaces. It analyzes both sequence-based and structure-based deep learning approaches, discussing the impact of protein language models (ESM, ProtTrans) and structural representations (graph neural networks, geometric deep learning) on prediction accuracy. Key advances including AlphaFold2-enabled structure prediction improving structure-based methods, multi-task learning across binding partner types, and generalization challenges are systematically reviewed. The paper provides a roadmap for future directions including foundation models for binding site prediction. The review highlights that geometric deep learning methods like MaSIF and PInet represent a new paradigm treating protein surfaces as manifolds, with surface-based descriptors outperforming either pure sequence or voxel-based 3D structure representations for binding site prediction.
datasets: Review of multiple benchmark datasets from PDB; BioLip, DUD-E, and specialized databases

title: Flex ddG: Rosetta ensemble-based estimation of changes in protein-protein binding affinity upon mutation
summary: Flex ddG is a Rosetta-based protocol for estimating interface ΔΔG upon mutation that explicitly samples conformational diversity using 'backrub' ensemble generation. Unlike static structure approaches, it generates an ensemble of protein conformations, applies torsion minimization and side chain repacking, and averages predictions across the ensemble to estimate binding affinity changes. Evaluated on 1,240 benchmark mutants from SKEMPI, Flex ddG outperforms existing methods—especially for small-to-large side chain changes, multiple simultaneous mutations, stabilizing mutations, and antibody-antigen interfaces. A non-linear re-weighting model using a generalized additive model (GAM) further improved agreement with experimental values, establishing a new benchmark for physics-based affinity change prediction.
datasets: SKEMPI, AB-Bind benchmark (1,240 mutants)

title: SKEMPI 2.0: an updated benchmark of changes in protein-protein binding energy, kinetics and thermodynamics upon mutation
summary: SKEMPI 2.0 is the gold-standard benchmark database for protein-protein binding affinity changes upon mutation. This major update to the SKEMPI database contains 7,085 manually curated mutational binding data points—an increase of 133% over the prior version—including kinetics for 1,844 mutations and thermodynamic data (enthalpy/entropy) for 443 mutations. The database encompasses structural, sequence, binding energy, kinetics, and thermodynamic information for structurally resolved protein-protein interactions. SKEMPI 2.0 has become the standard benchmark dataset used to train and evaluate virtually all computational methods for predicting ΔΔG upon PPI mutation, making it central to the field of binding affinity prediction.
datasets: SKEMPI 2.0 database (7,085 mutations across structurally resolved PPI complexes from PDB)

title: mCSM-PPI2: predicting the effects of mutations on protein-protein interactions
summary: mCSM-PPI2 is a machine learning method for predicting the effects of missense mutations on protein-protein interaction binding affinity. The method uses graph-based structural signatures to model changes in inter-residue interaction networks, combined with evolutionary information, complex network metrics, and energetic terms. It leverages physicochemical and geometrical properties of wild-type residue environments with substitution scores and normal mode analysis dynamics terms. Trained and validated on SKEMPI 2.0, mCSM-PPI2 demonstrated superior performance over previous methods in predicting both stabilizing and destabilizing mutations. The web server enables rapid assessment of mutation effects across diverse PPI complexes, making it widely used in antibody engineering and disease variant interpretation.
datasets: SKEMPI 2.0, AB-Bind

title: A topology-based network tree for the prediction of protein-protein binding affinity changes following mutation
summary: TopNetTree introduces a topology-based network tree method combining persistent homology-derived molecular descriptors with convolutional neural networks and gradient-boosting trees for predicting PPI binding affinity changes upon mutation. The approach represents protein-protein complexes using a topological characterization of the molecular interaction network, capturing multi-scale features at the interface. The integration of topological data analysis, element-specific persistent homology, and machine learning enables the model to encode spatial and chemical properties of protein interfaces beyond standard distance-based features. TopNetTree achieved state-of-the-art performance on SKEMPI and AB-Bind benchmarks, demonstrating the power of algebraic topology for molecular machine learning in affinity prediction tasks.
datasets: SKEMPI v1.0 and v2.0, AB-Bind

title: SSIPe: accurately estimating protein-protein binding affinity change upon mutations using evolutionary profiles in combination with an optimized physical energy function
summary: SSIPe (Sequence and Structural Information for PPI energy) combines protein interface profiles collected from structural and sequence homology searches with a physics-based energy function for accurate estimation of binding affinity changes (ΔΔGbind) upon mutation. To address statistical limitations of PPI structural databases, SSIPe uses amino acid-specific pseudocounts to prevent overfitting in sparse evolutionary profiles. The evolutionary interface profiles capture co-evolutionary constraints at protein interfaces, providing information beyond single-protein sequence conservation. SSIPe demonstrates competitive performance against state-of-the-art methods on SKEMPI and other benchmark datasets, particularly for mutations at interface residues with limited homologous structural data.
datasets: SKEMPI v2.0, PDB interface structural database

title: Deep geometric representations for modeling effects of mutations on protein-protein binding affinity (GeoPPI)
summary: GeoPPI learns geometric representations encoding topology features of protein structure via self-supervised pre-training on a large dataset of protein structures, then fine-tunes on labeled PPI binding affinity data. The learned geometric features characterize inter-atomic interactions and topology at the protein interface, providing a rich structural fingerprint for mutation effect prediction. These representations are combined with gradient-boosting trees for predicting binding affinity changes. Through extensive experiments, GeoPPI achieves new state-of-the-art performance on SKEMPI, demonstrating that geometry-aware self-supervised pre-training provides meaningful features for PPI affinity modeling. The approach validates using unlabeled structural data to improve predictions where labeled mutation data is scarce.
datasets: SKEMPI v1.0, SKEMPI v2.0, AB-Bind

title: Hom-Complex-Based Machine Learning (HCML) for the Prediction of PPI Binding Affinity Changes upon Mutation
venue: Journal of Chemical Information and Modeling
summary: HCML introduces the Hom-complex as a novel topological representation for protein-protein complexes and applies it to binding affinity change prediction upon mutation. Hom complexes Hom(G1, G) are constructed for the graph representation G of PPI complexes using various reference graphs G1, revealing G1-related inner connections within the complex structure. A series of nested Hom complexes provides multiscale characterization, with their persistent homology and Euler characteristics used as molecular descriptors. Combined with gradient boosting tree (GBT) models, HCML achieves state-of-the-art performance on SKEMPI and AB-Bind datasets, outperforming all existing models at publication. The method demonstrates algebraic topology's power for capturing complex geometric and topological relationships at PPI interfaces, with applications to antibody optimization for SARS-CoV-2.
datasets: SKEMPI v1.0 and v2.0, AB-Bind

title: Machine learning methods for protein-protein binding affinity prediction in protein design
summary: This comprehensive review systematically surveys machine learning methods for protein-protein binding affinity prediction in the context of protein design applications including antibody design, enzyme engineering, and biosensor construction. The paper categorizes approaches by input representation (sequence, structure, and combined methods), learning paradigm (supervised, semi-supervised, transfer learning), and application domain. A thorough analysis of available benchmark datasets—including SKEMPI, AB-Bind, and PPB—discusses their sizes, biases, and suitability for different prediction tasks. The review identifies key challenges: data scarcity, generalization to novel interfaces, and handling conformational flexibility. Practical guidance is provided for constructing effective affinity predictors for specific protein design applications.
datasets: SKEMPI, AB-Bind, PPB-Affinity (survey across multiple datasets)

title: DDMut-PPI: predicting effects of mutations on protein-protein interactions using graph-based deep learning
summary: DDMut-PPI is a deep learning model that efficiently predicts changes in PPI binding free energy (ΔΔG) upon single and multiple point mutations using a Siamese network architecture with graph-based interface signatures. The model is enhanced with a graph convolutional network operating on protein interaction interfaces and uses residue-specific embeddings from the ProtT5 protein language model to incorporate evolutionary context. DDMut-PPI achieves state-of-the-art performance on SKEMPI v2.0 benchmarks and demonstrates consistent performance across both stability-increasing and decreasing mutations—a common failure mode of prior methods. Available as a web server and API, it represents a fast and accurate tool suitable for high-throughput mutation scanning at PPI interfaces.
datasets: SKEMPI v2.0, independent validation sets

title: DeepPPAPredMut: deep ensemble method for predicting the binding affinity change in protein-protein complexes upon mutation
summary: DeepPPAPredMut is a deep ensemble model that leverages protein sequences, predicted structure-based features from AlphaFold2, and protein functional class annotations to predict binding affinity changes upon mutations in protein-protein complexes. The approach combines multiple complementary feature representations—including evolutionary information, structural descriptors from AlphaFold2-predicted structures, physicochemical properties, and functional classifications—to build a robust ensemble predictor. Evaluated on SKEMPI 2.0, the method achieves competitive performance and demonstrates the utility of combining sequence-based and structure-based features. The model's functional class stratification enables specialized predictions for antibody-antigen, enzyme-inhibitor, and other interaction categories.
datasets: SKEMPI v2.0, functional class-stratified benchmarks

title: Quantification of biases in predictions of protein-protein binding affinity changes upon mutations
summary: This critical analysis benchmarks eight widely-used PPI binding affinity change predictors—including BeAtMuSiC, mCSM-PPI2, FoldX, and others—and identifies systematic biases and dataset dependencies in their performance claims. The authors show that reported performance on SKEMPI 2.0 is overly optimistic due to training/test data overlap and biased mutation distributions toward interface residues. Evaluation on deep mutagenesis data from SARS-CoV-2 spike protein reveals substantially lower performance on unseen mutations. Key biases identified include antisymmetry violation (incorrect sign predictions for reverse mutations), overrepresentation of interface mutations in training, and limited generalization to out-of-distribution targets. This benchmark establishes critical evaluation standards for the field.
datasets: SKEMPI 2.0, SARS-CoV-2 spike DMS data

title: DDAffinity: predicting the changes in binding affinity of multiple point mutations using protein 3D structure
summary: DDAffinity proposes a spatial and sequential message passing neural network for predicting binding affinity changes caused by multiple point mutations using protein 3D structures. Unlike prior methods limited to single mutations, DDAffinity explicitly models multi-point mutation effects using local and global structural context. The spatial message passing captures geometric relationships in the 3D structure around mutation sites, while sequential message passing aggregates information along protein chains. The dual-pathway design enables the model to capture both local interface perturbations and distal structural effects. Evaluation on SKEMPI v1.0 and v2.0 demonstrates state-of-the-art performance for both single and multiple mutation scenarios, with practical utility for multi-residue engineering of antibody binding interfaces.
datasets: SKEMPI v1.0, SKEMPI v2.0

title: HADDOCK: a protein-protein docking approach based on biochemical or biophysical information
venue: Journal of the American Chemical Society
summary: HADDOCK (High Ambiguity Driven DOCKing) is a seminal information-driven protein docking method that uses biochemical or biophysical experimental data—including NMR chemical shift perturbations, mutagenesis data, SAXS, and bioinformatics interface predictions—to drive the docking process. Ambiguous Interaction Restraints (AIRs) are derived from this experimental information to constrain sampling and refinement in three docking stages: rigid body energy minimization, semi-flexible simulated annealing, and explicit water refinement. HADDOCK uniquely accepts any type of experimental data without requiring exact binding site knowledge. It has achieved consistently excellent performance in CAPRI over two decades and remains the preferred tool when experimental interface data is available, with over 50,000 registered users.
datasets: CAPRI benchmark targets, CCPN NMR data, mutagenesis data

title: ZDOCK server: interactive docking prediction of protein-protein complexes and symmetric multimers
summary: ZDOCK is a widely used Fast Fourier Transform-based protein-protein docking server implementing a pairwise shape complementarity, desolvation, and electrostatics scoring function. The ZDOCK 3.0 web server introduced interactive docking capabilities and support for symmetric homodimer and homo-oligomer prediction, addressing both heteromeric and symmetric complex structures. The server includes options for blocking interface residues and using known interface constraints to guide sampling. With optimizations using 3D convolution libraries, ZDOCK achieves efficient sampling of billions of rotational and translational conformations. As one of the most widely cited docking servers, ZDOCK benchmarks are a standard against which newer deep learning methods are evaluated.
datasets: Protein docking benchmarks 3.0 and 4.0, CAPRI targets

title: The ClusPro web server for protein-protein docking
summary: ClusPro is one of the most widely used automated web servers for protein-protein docking, implementing a three-step pipeline: FFT-based sampling of billions of conformations, PIPER-based energy minimization, and cluster-based model selection. The method was the top-performing server in multiple CAPRI rounds and has been used in hundreds of thousands of docking calculations. ClusPro 2.0 implements scoring based on four energy types (electrostatic, hydrophobic, van der Waals, buried surface area) and uses clustering to identify near-native solutions. This protocol paper provides a comprehensive guide to ClusPro usage and interpretation of results. As a classical server with consistently strong CAPRI performance, ClusPro remains a key benchmark comparator for new deep learning docking methods.
datasets: CAPRI benchmark targets, Docking Benchmark 4.0/5.0

title: HDOCK: a web server for protein-protein and protein-DNA/RNA docking based on a hybrid strategy
summary: HDOCK is a hybrid docking web server for protein-protein and protein-nucleic acid complex structure prediction, combining template-based and ab initio docking strategies. The hybrid approach uses sequence/structure homology to identify template complexes from PDB when available, and falls back to ab initio FFT-based shape complementarity when templates are absent. HDOCK uses a combined score incorporating shape complementarity, electrostatics, and atomic interactions. Tested on multiple benchmarks with weakly homologous complexes, HDOCK performs comparably to template-based modeling for protein-protein docking and outperforms it for protein-DNA complexes. With over one million docking calculations performed through its server, HDOCK is among the most widely used web servers in the structural biology community.
datasets: Protein docking benchmarks 4.0/5.0, CAPRI targets, protein-DNA benchmark

title: GalaxyTongDock: Symmetric and asymmetric ab initio protein-protein docking web server with improved energy parameters
summary: GalaxyTongDock is an ab initio protein-protein docking web server performing both asymmetric and symmetric docking with improved energy parameters trained by iterative optimization. The energy function was trained so that more native-like structures are selected as top-ranked predictions. GalaxyTongDock handles asymmetric docking (GalaxyTongDock_A), cyclic symmetry homo-oligomers (GalaxyTongDock_C), and dihedral symmetry complexes (GalaxyTongDock_D). On the unbound docking benchmark and symmetric model benchmark, performance is comparable to or better than ZDOCK. Experimental and evolutionary interface information can be incorporated via block and interface constraints, enabling integration of experimental data. The server provides an accessible platform for both rigid-body and symmetry-aware protein complex structure prediction.
datasets: Unbound docking benchmark, symmetric docking benchmark

title: Accurate and efficient protein structure prediction for biological sequences using RoseTTAFold
summary: RoseTTAFold introduces a three-track neural network architecture that simultaneously processes protein sequence information, inter-residue distance geometries, and 3D coordinates in an end-to-end fashion. The three tracks (1D sequence, 2D distance map, 3D coordinates) iteratively exchange information via transformer-based attention mechanisms, enabling bidirectional information flow. RoseTTAFold achieved near-AlphaFold2 accuracy on CASP14 benchmarks and introduced the ability to predict multi-chain protein complexes and protein-nucleic acid complexes. The architecture has been widely adopted as a basis for specialized models (RoseTTAFold-NA, RF-diffusion, and others), making it a foundational contribution to AI-based structural biology, including protein complex modeling.
datasets: CASP14 targets, PDB (70,000 non-redundant sequences), curated protein complex benchmarks

title: Protein complex prediction with AlphaFold-Multimer
summary: AlphaFold-Multimer is the landmark extension of AlphaFold to multi-chain protein complex structure prediction. Trained specifically on multimeric inputs of known stoichiometry, it introduces paired multiple sequence alignments (MSAs) across chains to capture inter-chain evolutionary covariation. On a 17-heterodimer benchmark without templates, AlphaFold-Multimer achieves DockQ ≥ 0.49 on 13 targets and DockQ ≥ 0.8 on 7 targets. For 4,446 recent protein complexes, it successfully predicts 70% of heteromeric interfaces (DockQ ≥ 0.23) and 26% at high accuracy (DockQ ≥ 0.8)—representing +27 and +14 percentage point improvements over prior AlphaFold approaches. This paper defines the current baseline for AI-based protein complex structure prediction.
datasets: PDB complexes benchmark (4,446 recent complexes), 17-heterodimer no-template benchmark

title: Benchmarking AlphaFold for protein complex modeling reveals accuracy determinants
summary: This early benchmarking study explores AlphaFold (non-multimer) for protein complex structure prediction using 152 diverse heterodimeric complexes. Multiple AlphaFold implementations and parameter settings were tested for accuracy in predicting complex structures from sequence. Remarkably, many cases yielded highly accurate top-ranked predictions, greatly surpassing the performance of conventional unbound protein-protein docking. However, antibody-antigen docking remained largely unsuccessful, highlighting a domain-specific limitation. The study identifies accuracy determinants including template availability, co-evolutionary signal strength, and interface size. This work established AlphaFold as a powerful tool for complex structure prediction and revealed the ongoing challenge of antibody-antigen modeling, motivating specialized antibody complex prediction methods.
datasets: 152 heterodimeric protein complexes from PDB, antibody-antigen complex subset

title: DiffDock-PP: Rigid Protein-Protein Docking with Diffusion Models
summary: DiffDock-PP adapts the diffusion generative model framework for rigid protein-protein docking, learning to translate and rotate unbound protein structures into their bound conformations. The model operates over the non-Euclidean manifold of rigid-body transformations (SO(3) × R3), iteratively refining pose predictions through a learned reverse diffusion process. DiffDock-PP achieves state-of-the-art performance on DIPS with a median C-RMSD of 4.85 Å, outperforming all considered baselines including traditional FFT-based methods. It is significantly faster than search-based methods and generates reliable confidence estimates for predictions. This work extends the successful DiffDock paradigm from small molecule docking to protein-protein docking, opening new avenues for generative complex structure prediction.
datasets: DIPS (Database of Interacting Protein Structures), Docking Benchmark 5.5

title: Enhancing AlphaFold-Multimer-based protein complex structure prediction with MULTICOM in CASP15
summary: MULTICOM is an ensemble system that enhances AlphaFold-Multimer performance through three strategies: diverse MSA sampling using both traditional sequence alignments and novel Foldseek structure alignments, multi-metric model ranking, and structure alignment-based refinement. Evaluated in CASP15 as both server and human predictor, MULTICOM_qa ranked 3rd among 26 server predictors with an average TM-score of 0.76 versus 0.72 for standard AlphaFold-Multimer—a 5.3% improvement. The Foldseek Structure Alignment-based Multimer Generation (FSAMG) method outperforms sequence alignment-based generation for structurally diverse complexes. This work demonstrates that optimizing the input to AlphaFold-Multimer (MSA diversity and template selection) is as important as model architecture advances.
datasets: CASP15 assembly targets, PDB complex structures

title: Flexible protein-protein docking with a multitrack iterative transformer (GeoDock)
summary: GeoDock is a multi-track iterative transformer network for flexible protein-protein docking that inputs protein sequences and structures (without requiring MSAs) and directly predicts docked complex structures with residue-level backbone flexibility. Unlike rigid-body deep learning methods, GeoDock allows conformational changes upon binding, addressing allosteric inhibition and uncertain unbound structures. For rigid docking targets, GeoDock achieves a 41% success rate, outperforming all other tested deep learning methods. For flexible targets, performance is comparable to the traditional ClusPro server. With inference under one second per complex on a single GPU, GeoDock enables large-scale structural screening at throughput inaccessible to traditional docking methods.
datasets: Docking Benchmark 5.5 (DB5.5), flexible docking targets

title: Improving deep learning protein monomer and complex structure prediction using DeepMSA2
summary: DeepMSA2 is a pipeline for constructing deep and diverse multiple sequence alignments (MSAs) for protein structure prediction, leveraging iterative alignment search against large genomic and metagenomic databases including BFD, Uniclust30, and Giant Metagenomic databases. The pipeline demonstrates that MSA quality is a critical bottleneck for complex structure prediction—providing high-quality MSAs to AlphaFold-Multimer significantly improves complex structure accuracy compared to standard MSA construction. In CASP15, an integrated pipeline using DeepMSA2 substantially outperformed the AlphaFold2-Multimer server (v.2.2.0) for complex modeling. The work demonstrates that optimization of input information to deep learning structure predictors is as important as model architecture improvements.
datasets: CASP15 complex targets, PDB complex benchmarks, BFD/Uniclust30/metagenomics databases

title: HelixFold-Multimer: Elevating Protein Complex Structure Prediction to New Heights
summary: HelixFold-Multimer is an advanced protein complex structure prediction model that achieves substantially improved performance over AlphaFold-Multimer, particularly for therapeutic protein interactions and antigen-antibody complexes. The model excels in cross-species complexes from different organisms—a key challenge where standard AlphaFold-Multimer often fails due to limited paired MSA availability. HelixFold-Multimer achieves remarkable success in antigen-antibody and peptide-protein structure prediction, surpassing AlphaFold 3 on these challenging benchmarks. Available on the PaddleHelix platform in general and antigen-antibody specific versions, the model provides an accessible interface for therapeutic antibody complex modeling and protein-peptide interaction prediction.
datasets: Antigen-antibody benchmark complexes, peptide-protein benchmark

title: Revisiting Protein-Protein Docking: A Systematic Evaluation Framework
venue: Journal of Chemical Information and Modeling
summary: This systematic benchmarking study evaluates 11 protein-protein docking methods—traditional (HDOCK, PatchDock, PIPER, ZDOCK) and deep learning (EquiDock, ElliDock, EBMDock, GeoDock, DiffDock-PP, AlphaFold-Multimer, AlphaFold3)—using three datasets: Docking Benchmark 5.5 for flexible docking, AACBench for antibody-antigen docking, and PPCBench for out-of-distribution generalization. AlphaFold3 achieves a superior top-5 success rate of 77.98% against apo structures and 31.78% for antibody-antigen docking. Traditional HDOCK achieves 85.24% with holo structures but only 12.84% with apo structures. Critically, all DL-based models show markedly reduced performance on the OOD PPCBench, indicating generalization challenges for deep learning docking approaches.
datasets: Docking Benchmark 5.5, AACBench (antibody-antigen), PPCBench (OOD)

title: Computational approaches for prediction of pathogen-host protein-protein interactions
summary: This foundational review systematically covers computational approaches for predicting pathogen-host protein-protein interactions (PHIs), distinguishing methods based on sequence, structure, and known interaction information. It reviews classic machine learning approaches (SVM, Naive Bayes, Random Forest) applicable when training data is available, and transfer/multitask learning methods for data-scarce settings. The paper analyzes challenges specific to host-pathogen prediction: limited experimental data, highly heterogeneous host-pathogen pairs, molecular mimicry as a predictive feature, and negative sample selection issues. The review provides foundational context for subsequent deep learning approaches to HP-PPI prediction and remains a key reference for the field.
datasets: HPIDB, HIV-human, Yersinia-human, and other host-pathogen PPI datasets

title: HPiP: an R/Bioconductor package for predicting host-pathogen protein-protein interactions from protein sequences using ensemble machine learning
summary: HPiP is an R/Bioconductor software package for predicting host-pathogen PPIs from protein sequences using an ensemble of machine learning classifiers. The package implements a series of amino acid sequence property descriptors including physicochemical, evolutionary, and structural features, combined with ensemble classifiers (Random Forest, SVM, XGBoost) to predict unmapped interactions. Using SARS-CoV-1 and SARS-CoV-2 coronavirus-human PPI training sets, HPiP achieves good performance in identifying novel interactions. The package provides standardized preprocessing, model training, and prediction pipelines accessible within the Bioconductor ecosystem, enabling researchers without deep machine learning expertise to conduct HP-PPI prediction analyses.
datasets: SARS-CoV-1 and SARS-CoV-2 human PPI datasets, HPIDB

title: Fast, accurate antibody structure prediction from deep learning on massive set of natural antibodies (IgFold)
summary: IgFold is a fast deep learning method for antibody structure prediction using an end-to-end architecture consisting of a pre-trained antibody language model (trained on 558 million natural antibody sequences) followed by graph networks that directly predict backbone atom coordinates. IgFold predicts structures of similar or better quality than alternative methods including AlphaFold in under 25 seconds per structure. This speed enables previously infeasible analyses: the authors predicted structures for 1.4 million paired antibody sequences, providing structural insights to 500-fold more antibodies than have experimental structures. IgFold's speed-accuracy balance makes it the preferred tool for high-throughput antibody structural analysis and repertoire profiling.
datasets: Observed Antibody Space (OAS), 558M antibody sequences, SAbDab structural benchmark

title: ImmuneBuilder: Deep-Learning models for predicting the structures of immune proteins
summary: ImmuneBuilder is a set of deep learning models for accurate structure prediction of immune proteins: ABodyBuilder2 for antibodies, NanoBodyBuilder2 for nanobodies, and TCRBuilder2 for T-cell receptors. The models achieve state-of-the-art accuracy while being substantially faster than AlphaFold2—processing an antibody in ~4 seconds versus ~20 minutes. Each model provides per-residue error estimates, enabling confidence-based filtering and selection. ABodyBuilder2's specialized antibody-specific architecture and training data produces more accurate CDR loop predictions compared to general protein structure predictors, particularly for the challenging CDR-H3 loop which is critical for antigen binding. ImmuneBuilder has become the standard fast method for high-throughput immune protein structural analysis.
datasets: SAbDab (Structural Antibody Database), Protein Data Bank antibody structures

title: Towards the accurate modelling of antibody-antigen complexes from sequence using machine learning and information-driven docking
summary: This paper analyzes the ability of protein-protein docking tools to use machine learning-generated antibody structures for information-driven docking of antibody-antigen complexes. HADDOCK combined with an ensemble of ML-predicted antibody structures (IgFold, ImmuneBuilder, AlphaFold2) and AlphaFold2-predicted antigen structures achieves accurate antibody-antigen complex models. Targeted docking using CDR region knowledge and epitope information enables high-quality models with reduced sampling, providing a computationally efficient protocol that outperforms the ZDOCK baseline. The study demonstrates that combining ML structure prediction with information-driven docking provides a practical workflow for therapeutic antibody complex modeling when experimental structures are unavailable.
